### SEO Description

**First 2 Sentences (CRITICAL — shown in search & above fold):**
> If climbing two flights of stairs leaves you winded after 60, it's not just "getting older." There's a specific muscle in your calf — called the soleus — that acts as a second heart, pumping blood back up from your legs. When it weakens, blood pools in your legs, your brain gets less oxygen, and your heart has to work overtime.

**Full Description (recommended length: 250-300 words):**
```
If climbing two flights of stairs leaves you winded after 60, it's not just "getting older." There's a specific muscle in your calf — called the soleus — that acts as a second heart, pumping blood back up from your legs. When it weakens, blood pools in your legs, your brain gets less oxygen, and your heart has to work overtime.

Dr. Victor Kane explains the vascular pump mechanism that most doctors never mention — and why the standard "just walk more" advice misses the point entirely.

In this video, you'll learn:
• Why the soleus muscle is different from every other muscle in your leg (it's designed for endurance, not power)
• The specific fiber-type distinction that makes your calf a "peripheral heart"
• Why generic leg exercises DON'T activate the vascular pump
• The 3-exercise soleus pump protocol that targets venous return specifically
• How to test your own soleus function at home in 60 seconds

📋 CHAPTERS:
0:00 — The woman who couldn't climb 12 stairs
1:30 — The soleus: your body's second heart
3:15 — Why Type I muscle fibers matter after 60
5:00 — The venous valve system most people never hear about
7:30 — The 60-second soleus self-test
9:15 — The 3-exercise pump protocol
12:00 — What to ask your doctor about vascular function

⭐ Subscribe to Dr. Kane's channel for vascular health insights your doctor may not explain — covering stroke prevention, blood pressure, circulation, and the hidden mechanisms behind aging.

📌 Related Videos:

#VascularHealth #Over60Health #Circulation #SoleusPump #LegHealth #StrokePrevention
```

### SEO Description

**First 2 Sentences (CRITICAL — shown in search & above fold):**
> Most people think strokes come without warning. But your brain sends signals — sometimes weeks before — that most people ignore or mistake for something else. Dr. Victor Kane explains the 4 warning patterns that could save your life.

**Full Description (recommended length: 250-300 words):**
```
Most people think strokes come without warning. But your brain sends signals — sometimes weeks before — that most people ignore or mistake for something else. Dr. Victor Kane explains the 4 warning patterns that could save your life.

This isn't about FAST (Face, Arms, Speech, Time). This is about the micro-signals your brain sends weeks before a major event — and why standard checkups aren't designed to catch them.

In this video, you'll learn:
• The 4 stroke warning patterns: what they look like and why they're easy to dismiss
• The micro-emboli mechanism: how tiny blood clots trigger warning events
• Why TIAs ("mini-strokes") are often missed or misdiagnosed in adults over 60
• The exact self-assessment questions to ask yourself every morning
• Which cardiovascular tests actually detect stroke risk (and which ones don't)
• A word-for-word script for discussing stroke risk with your doctor

📋 CHAPTERS:
0:00 — Robert's 4 warnings (patient story)

4:30 — Warning Pattern #1: Transient visual disturbances
6:15 — Warning Pattern #2: One-sided sensory changes
8:00 — Warning Pattern #3: Speech gaps and word-finding failures
10:00 — Warning Pattern #4: Balance and spatial disorientation
12:00 — The daily 2-minute self-check after 60
14:00 — What to tell your doctor (exact script)
16:00 — Robert's outcome: "I caught mine in time"

⭐ Subscribe for vascular and brain health from a doctor who explains the mechanisms — not just the symptoms.

📌 Related Videos:

→ Heartbeat Patterns and Stroke Risk (VID-021)

#StrokePrevention #Over60Health #StrokeWarningSigns #TIAAwareness #BrainHealth #VascularNeurology
```
